8: yiqqāhēl kol-rᵉkûšô — 'all his property shall be forfeited' (10:8). The verb ḥāram underlies the legal formula here (NIV 'forfeited'; lit. 'devoted to destruction/ban'). The ḥerem principle — the consecration of property to YHWH by exclusion from ordinary use — is being applied to covenant defaulters: failure to appear at the assembly within three days would result in the forfeiture of property and exclusion from the congregation of exiles. The application of the ḥerem logic to property in a covenant-discipline context is unusual — it transfers the war-spoils vocabulary of Deuteronomy into a post-exilic communal governance register. The 'congregation of the exiles' (qᵉhal haggôlāh) is itself a coinage — the exilic community as the new covenant assembly.
11: hiḇḏalû mikᵉʿammê hāʾāreṣ — 'separate yourselves from the peoples of the land' (10:11). The Nip'al imperative of bāḏal mirrors the diagnosis of 9:1 (lōʾ-hiḇḏalû) in its solution: the covenant violation was not-separating; the covenant restoration is separating. The bāḏal vocabulary connects the entire Ezra 9-10 unit into a chiastic pair. The phrase ʿammê hāʾāreṣ (peoples of the land) is the Ezra-Nehemiah technical term for the non-exilic, mixed-population indigenous peoples — not identical with the Pentateuchal 'Canaanites' but invoking the same covenantal boundary. The dissolution of the foreign marriages (nāšîm nᵉḵrîyyôt, foreign women) is the practical enactment of bāḏal — the vocabulary of separation made domestic and personal.
1: ûkᵉhiṯwaddōṯ ʿezrāʾ ûkᵉhiṯpallᵉlô — “while Ezra was confessing and praying.” Two simultaneous participles: hiṯwaddōṯ (Hitpael of yādāh, to confess) and hiṯpallēl (Hitpael of pālal, to pray/intercede). The Hitpael of yādāh specifically means to make public confession of sin — the same construction used in Lev 16:21 (the High Priest’s confessing of Israel’s sins). Ezra’s prostration (mippānāyw — “before him”, i.e., God’s house) draws a vast congregation of men, women, and children weeping with great weeping (bᵉḵî rabba). His individual intercession becomes communal.
2: wayyaʿan šeḵanyāh ben-yᵉḥîʾēl — “Shechaniah son of Jehiel responded.” The verb ʿānāh (to respond/answer) here has the specific nuance of stepping forward into a moment of crisis. Shechaniah’s family is itself implicated — his kinsmen of Elam are in the list at 10:26. His willingness to lead the repentance despite personal cost gives the speech authority. The confession is direct: māʿalnû bēʾlōhênû — “we have been unfaithful to our God” — using the technical term māʿal (unfaithfulness, sacrilege) from the cultic vocabulary (Lev 5:15; Num 5:12). The remedy proposed is equally direct: covenant renewal.
3: wᵉʿattāh nikkrōt-bᵉrît lēʾlōhênû — “now let us make a covenant with our God.” The phrase kāraṯ bᵉrît (to cut a covenant) is the standard idiom for covenant-making in biblical Hebrew, perhaps derived from the practice of cutting an animal in covenant ratification (Gen 15:10). The covenant’s content: to send away (lᵉhôṣîʾ) all the foreign wives and their children. Shechaniah grounds this in two authorities: kᵉʿaṣat ʾăḏōnāy — “according to the counsel of the Lord” — and those who tremble at the commandments (haḥărēḏîm bᵉmiṣwaṯ ʾĕlōhênû). The ḥărēḏîm (“tremblers”) are a post-exilic piety group whose zeal for the Torah is their identifying mark (Isa 66:2, 5).
4: qûm kî ʿāleḵā haddāḇār — “rise up, for this matter belongs to you.” Shechaniah’s charge to Ezra uses the imperative qûm (rise) — the classic call to decisive action (1 Kgs 19:5; Dan 8:27). The phrase ʿāleḵā haddāḇār means literally “the matter is upon you” — responsibility is formally transferred to Ezra’s shoulders. The accompanying promise — wᵉʾănaḥnû ʿimmāḵ ḥăzaq waʿăśēh — “we are with you, be strong and act” — echoes the Deuteronomic commission formulas (Deut 31:7; Josh 1:6). The community’s solidarity with the leader is explicitly pledged.
5: wayyāqom ʿezrāʾ wayyaššāḇaʿ ʾeṯ-śārê haKKōhănîm — “then Ezra rose and put the leading priests, Levites, and all Israel under oath.” The verb šāḇaʿ (Hiphil, to cause to swear) is the covenant oath-administration verb. The subjects: leading priests (śārê haKKōhănîm), Levites (haLLᵉwiyyim), all Israel (kol-yiśrāʾēl). All three orders of the covenant community are bound by the oath — no one is exempt. The phrase laʿăśôṯ kadDāḇār hazzeh — “to act according to this matter” — keeps the commitment specific and actionable.
6: wayyāqom ʿezrāʾ millipnê bêṯ hāʾĕlōhîm — “Ezra rose from before the house of God.” He moves to the chamber of Jehohanan son of Eliashib (liška yᵉhôḥānān ben-ʾelyāšîḇ). Jehohanan appears in the Elephantine papyri (ca. 407 BCE) as a high priest, which may help date the narrative. Ezra’s night fast — lᵉḥem lōʾ ʾāḵal ûmayim lōʾ šātāh — is the total fast of grief, not merely the cultic fast of humiliation. His mourning is for māʿal haggôlāh — “the unfaithfulness of the exile-community” — the same māʿal as in v.2.
7: wayyaʿăḇîrû qôl bîhûdāh ûḇîrûšālaim — “a proclamation was issued throughout Judah and Jerusalem.” The term qayyaʿăḇîrû qôl (to pass a sound/voice through) is the standard idiom for official proclamation (2 Chr 30:5; Neh 8:15). The directive is urgent: hiqqāḇᵉṣû — “assemble” — within three days, to Jerusalem. The penalty clause is severe (kol-ʾăšer lōʾ-yāḇōʾ litᵉlōšeṯ hayyāmîm — “anyone who fails to appear within three days”) — forfeiture of property and exclusion from the community of the returned exiles.
9: wayyiqqāḇᵉṣû kol-ʾanšê yᵉhûdāh ûḇinyāmîn — “all the men of Judah and Benjamin gathered.” The two southern tribes form the core of the restoration community. The gathering is dated: the twentieth day of the ninth month (Kislev, approximately December). The setting — Jerusalem plaza, in front of God’s house — is the sacred assembly space; the weather — heavy rain (haggᵉšāmîm) — intensifies the urgency. The people trembled (mērāʿîm) from both the rain and the gravity of the matter (ʿal-haddāḇār). The double cause of trembling — weather and covenant — is not separated.
10: wayyāqom ʿezrāʾ hakKōhēn wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem — “Ezra the priest stood and said to them.” The charge is crisp and in three movements: (1) ʾaṯem māʿaltem — “you have been unfaithful”; (2) wattōšîḇû nāšîm nāḵriyyôṯ — “you have married foreign women”; (3) lᵉhôsîp ʿal-ʾašmaṯ yiśrāʾēl — “adding to the guilt of Israel.” The verb yāsap (to add) is damning — the intermarriages are not merely individual sins but incremental additions to a national debt that has already nearly destroyed the community.
12: wayyaʿan kol-haqqāhāl wayyōʾmᵉrû qôl gādôl — “the whole assembly answered with a loud voice.” The communal response is unanimous and vocal: kēn kidᵉḇāreḵā ʿālênû laʿăśôṯ — “yes, we must do exactly as you have said.” The adverb kēn (thus/so) is the covenant acceptance formula — straightforward assent. laʿăśôṯ (to do/act) is the active commitment word. The unanimity is rhetorically significant — no dissenting voice delays the covenant renewal. The loud voice (qôl gādôl) signals public, accountable, communal commitment, not private individual decision.
13: ʾăḇāl hāʿām rāḇ wᵉhāʿēṯ gᵉšāmîm — “however, there are many people involved, and it is the rainy season.” The practical objection comes from within the assembly. The rain makes outdoor standing impossible for an extended period; and the matter requires individual case review (ḥāṭāʾnû baddāḇār hazzeh — “we have sinned in this matter” is acknowledged even by those requesting an orderly process). The objection is not resistance to repentance but to rushed implementation. The verb ʿāmad (to stand) in the rain for mass processing is impractical — the covenant community needs a structured investigation process.
14: yaʿōmᵉḏû nāʾ śārênû lᵉḵol-haqqāhāl — “let our officials represent the whole assembly.” The proposed solution is administrative: officials (śārîm) act as assembly representatives; all who have intermarried come before them on fixed timetables with the elders and judges of their cities (ʿim-ziqnê ʿîr-wāʿîr wᵉšōpᵉṭêhā). The purpose: lᵉhāšîḇ ḥărôn ʾap-ʾĕlōhênû — “to turn away the fierce wrath of our God.” The phrase ḥărôn ʾap (burning anger) is the standard OT expression for imminent divine judgment; the assembly understands the urgency.
15: ʾaḵ yônatān ben-ʾăśāhēl wᵉyaḥzeyāh ben-tiqwāh — only Jonathan son of Asahel and Jahzeiah son of Tikvah opposed the plan, supported by Meshullam and Shabbethai the Levite. The dissent is noted precisely — names, not merely a number. The text does not explain their objection (perhaps they considered the divorce requirement too harsh, or disputed the investigation procedure). Their opposition is overruled, but the fact of recording it shows the transparency of the process: covenant renewal in Ezra is not unanimity by suppression but by the weight of acknowledged obligation.
16: wayyiḇḏᵉlû ʾanšê-haggôlāh — “the returned exiles separated themselves.” Ezra designates men — heads of families (rāšê hāʾāḇôṯ), all named — to serve as the investigation committee. They began on the first day of the tenth month (ḥōdeš hāʿăśîrî, Tevet — approximately January). The verb yāšaḇ (they sat) is the technical term for a judicial sitting; the committee hears each case to determine its disposition. The four-month process reflects the thoroughness required: each case must be evaluated individually.
17: waykallû ʾeṯ-kol-hāʾănāšîm — “by the first day of the first month they had completed their review.” The review took three months (tenth month to first month of the following year). The verb kālāh (Piel, to complete/finish) marks the satisfactory conclusion. The object: kol-hāʾănāšîm ʾăšer-hôšîḇû nāšîm nāḵriyyôṯ — “all the men who had taken foreign women.” The systematic thoroughness is itself a covenantal statement: no one is above covenant accountability, no case is too inconvenient to investigate.
18: wayyimmāṣēʾ mibbᵉnê haKKōhănîm — “among the sons of the priests the following were found.” The list of violators begins with the priesthood — the highest sacred order is implicated first. The sons of Jeshua (the High Priest’s family, bᵉnê-yēšûaʿ ben-yôṣādāq wᵉ'eḥāyw) head the list. This is the most prominent priestly family in the restoration community. Their violation of the intermarriage prohibition is the most serious possible failure, given that priestly genealogical purity was a fundamental requirement for temple service (Lev 21:7).
19: wayyittᵉnû yādām lᵉhôṣîʾ nᵉšêhem — “they pledged to put away their wives.” The idiom nāṯan yāḏ (to give the hand) is a gesture of formal pledge or commitment — a handshake covenant (1 Chr 29:24; 2 Chr 30:8). The priests acknowledge their guilt (wᵉʾăšēmîm) and bring an atonement offering: a ram (ʾayil ṣōʾn) for their sin. The guilt offering for priestly unfaithfulness is calibrated to the seriousness of the violation — a ram is the minimum offering for a ruler’s guilt offering (Lev 5:18).
20: ûmibbᵉnê ʾimmēr — “from the sons of Immer.” Immer was one of the twenty-four priestly divisions established by David (1 Chr 24:14). Two men are named: Hanani and Zebadiah. The list continues through the major priestly divisions: Immer, Harim, and Pashur (vv.20-22) — all prominent priestly families in the restoration period. The precise enumeration within each priestly division demonstrates that the investigation was conducted systematically, division by division.
21: ûmibbᵉnê ḥārîm — “from the sons of Harim.” Five violators are listed from this division: Maaseiah, Elijah, Shemaiah, Jehiel, and Uzziah. The Harim division appears in Ezra 2:32, 39 and Neh 7:35, 42 as a significant priestly and lay family group. The theophoric names among the violators — Elijah (“my God is YHWH”), Shemaiah (“YHWH has heard”) — underline the irony: those whose very names confess covenant loyalty are the ones who violated it.
22: ûmibbᵉnê pašḥûr — “from the sons of Pashur.” Six names are listed from this division — the largest priestly contingent in the list. Pashur was a priestly division (1 Chr 9:12) as well as a specific individual known from Jer 20:1 as the priest who put Jeremiah in stocks. The list’s exhaustive naming protocol serves covenant accountability: violation of the intermarriage prohibition was a public matter whose resolution required public record.
23: ûmin-halLᵉwiyyim — “of the Levites.” Six Levites are named: Jozabad, Shimei, Kelaiah (also known as Kelita — the parenthetical alternative name is a textual note, probably reflecting a variant in the Levitical rolls), Pethahiah, Judah, and Eliezer. The Levites are listed separately from the priests, reflecting the hierarchical distinction maintained throughout the post-exilic period. The smaller number of Levitical violators (six) compared with the priestly violators may reflect the smaller Levitical population in the return community.
24: ûmin-hammᵉšôrᵉrîm ʾelyāšîḇ — “of the singers: Eliashib.” A single singer is named. ûmin-haššôʿărîm šallûm wāṭelem wāʾûrî — “of the gatekeepers: Shallum, Telem, and Uri.” These are the two lowest-ranking Levitical service orders (singers and gatekeepers). Their inclusion in the accountability process demonstrates that no level of the temple service structure was exempt. The gatekeepers in particular were the guards of the boundary between sacred and profane — their violation of the marriage boundary carries symbolic irony.
25: ûmiyyiśrāʾēl mibbᵉnê parʿōš — “among the lay Israelites, from the sons of Parosh.” The lay list is the most extensive section (vv.25-43), covering 17 different family groups. Parosh heads the list, as in Ezra 2:3, where the sons of Parosh are the largest single family group (2,172). The names within the Parosh contingent include compound forms: Malluchijah (“YHWH is my king”), Mijamin (a shortened theophoric). The variation in spelling across different manuscripts and parallel texts (Neh 10) reflects the challenges of maintaining consistent records in the complex textual history of Ezra-Nehemiah.
26: ûmibbᵉnê ʿêlām — “from the sons of Elam.” Elam is represented again (cf. 8:7) — a major family group in the return. Six names: Mattaniah, Zechariah, Jehiel, Abdi, Jeremoth, and Elijah. The prevalence of the name Mattaniah (“gift of YHWH”) across different family groups in this list suggests it was a popular name in the restoration community — the sentiment of grateful reception of divine gifts was a natural expression for those who had returned from exile.
27: ûmibbᵉnê zattûʾ — “from the sons of Zattu.” Six names from this family group (Zattu appears at Ezra 2:8 with 945 members). The last in the list, Aziza, carries the Arabic-influenced name-form common in the trans-Euphrates region — evidence of cultural contact in the diaspora. The mixed name-forms (Hebrew theophoric + Arabic/Aramaic) across the list reflect the multilingual, multicultural context of Babylonian Jewish life.
28: ûmibbᵉnê bēḇay — “from the sons of Bebai.” Four names from the smaller Bebai family group (cf. 8:11). Jehohanan, Hananiah, Zabbai, and Athlai. The name Jehohanan (“YHWH is gracious”) appears multiple times across these lists as one of the most common post-exilic names — the ḥannûn (gracious) root in divine names reflects the community’s experience of divine grace in the return from exile.
29: ûmibbᵉnê bānî — “from the sons of Bani.” Six names from Bani, including the common post-exilic name Meshullam (“at peace”) and Malluch (“counselor/king”). The name Adaiah (“YHWH has adorned/testified”) appears in multiple family groups across the list. The Bani family (cf. Ezra 2:10; 10:34, 38) was one of the larger lay groups with multiple sub-groups — their presence here across different sections of the list suggests that “Bani” may be a broad clan designation covering several related households.
30: ûmibbᵉnê paḥaṯ-môʾāḇ — “from the sons of Pahathmoab.” Eight names — the largest non-priestly contingent in the list. The clan name Pahathmoab (“governor of Moab”) suggests descent from an official who had governed Moabite territory under the Davidic-Solomonic administration; their large numbers in the returns (Ezra 2:6 — 2,812) indicate a major family group. The final name Manasseh (mᵉnašseh) completes the eight.
31: ûmibbᵉnê ḥārîm — the lay sons of Harim (distinct from the priestly sons of Harim at v.21). Five names: Eliezer, Ishijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon. The repetition of family names (Harim, Bani, Elam) across different sections of the list — priestly, Levitical, lay — reflects the extended family structure of the restoration community where priestly and lay branches of the same ancestral name coexisted.
32: binyāmîn mallûk wišmaryāh — three more from the Harim group: Benjamin, Malluch, and Shemariah. The list continues methodically without comment. The prose neutrality of the inventory — no moral commentary, no theological elaboration — is itself significant: the text treats covenant accountability as a practical matter requiring documentation, not a dramatic occasion requiring homiletical embellishment.
33: mibbᵉnê ḥāšum — “from the sons of Hashum.” Seven names including Mattenai, Mattattah, Zabad, Eliphelet, Jeremai, Manasseh, and Shimei. The Hashum family appears at Ezra 2:19 (223 members). The variety of name-forms across the seven (Aramaic-influenced Mattenai + Hebrew Eliphelet + Hebrew-Aramaic Jeremai) reflects the linguistic blending characteristic of the Babylonian diaspora community.
34: mibbᵉnê bānî — a second Bani contingent, or the beginning of the Bani sub-groups that extend through v.38. The names here are less common: Maadai, Amram (a patriarchal name — borne by Moses’ father, Exod 6:18), Uel. The recurrence of ancestral names (Amram) in the post-exilic period reflects deliberate naming practices meant to anchor the restoration community in the patriarchal narrative.
35: bᵉdᵉyāh kᵉlûhû wanᵉyāh — three more from the extended Bani group: Bedeiah, Chelluh (variant forms in ancient versions), Vaniah. These names are rare — several appear only here in the OT. Their rarity does not diminish their significance; the list preserves the names of individuals who accepted covenant accountability, many of whom would otherwise be entirely unknown. The OT’s preservation of obscure names in accountability lists reflects a theology of individual covenant responsibility.
36: mᵉrēmôṯ ʾelyāšîḇ — Meremoth and Eliashib. Meremoth appears as both a priest (Ezra 8:33; Neh 3:4) and a lay person in the tradition, which may indicate different individuals bearing the same name, or that this Meremoth is distinct from the priest of ch.8. Eliashib is extremely common in post-exilic texts (also the name of the High Priest in Neh 13:4). The short entries in vv.36-43 reflect the scribal economy of the list format — each name carries its own full accountability without need of commentary.
37: matnay waMattatnāh wîyāśāy — Mattenai, Mattaniah, and Jaasau. The name Mattenai (mattᵉnay — “gift of YHWH”) and Mattaniah (mattanyāh — the longer form with the divine element) are name variants of the same root — both meaning “gift of God.” Their sequential listing may reflect scribal grouping of name-variants. Jaasau (wîyāśāy) is a hapax — appearing only here — likely a contracted form of a theophoric name.
38: binnûy šimʿî — Binnui and Shimei. Binnui is a name associated with the Levitical family groups (Neh 3:18; 10:9; 12:8) but here appears in the lay list, suggesting it was a name used across different family groups. Shimei (šimʿî — “YHWH has heard”) is one of the most common OT names, appearing in dozens of contexts from the Davidic court onward. The frequency of theophoric names across all sections of this list reflects the persistence of YHWH-devotion in the diaspora community.
39: šelemyāh wᵉnātān wᵉʿăḏāyāh — Shelemiah, Nathan, and Adaiah. Shelemiah (šelemyāh — “YHWH has restored/completed”) and Nathan (the prophet’s name, meaning “YHWH has given”) and Adaiah (“YHWH has adorned”) round out another set of theophoric names. Nathan in the post-exilic context is a common name; the prophet Nathan played a significant role in the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7; 12), and his name was preserved in the royal line (Zech 12:12).
40: maknaḏᵉḇay šāšay šārāy — Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai. These are unusual names — Machnadebai appears only here in the OT. The name may be of non-Hebrew derivation, reflecting the cultural mixing of the Babylonian diaspora. Shashai and Sharai have uncertain etymologies. Their uniqueness is no barrier to their inclusion in the covenant accountability record — the list’s completeness requires naming every individual, regardless of the name’s frequency or recognizability.
41: ʿăzarʾēl wšelemyāh šemaryāh — Azareel, Shelemiah (a second occurrence), Shemariah. Azareel (ʿăzarʾēl — “God has helped”) appears in 1 Chr 12:6 as a Benjaminite warrior who joined David. The recurrence of Shelemiah in the same extended name-group (cf. v.39) may reflect sub-branch naming within the Bani family, or scribal variation in the source document.
42: šallûm ʾămaryāh wîyôsēp — Shallum, Amariah, and Joseph. Shallum is the classic gatekeeper name (Neh 7:45; 1 Chr 9:17); here it appears in a lay group. Amariah (ʾămaryāh — “YHWH has spoken/promised”) is a priestly name in Chronicles but lay here. Joseph closes the Bani sub-group — a patriarchal name, perhaps deliberately chosen to link the family’s identity with the foundational patriarch who himself spent years in exile in Egypt.
43: mibbᵉnê nᵉḇô — “from the sons of Nebo.” Seven names: Jeiel, Mattithiah, Zabad, Zebina, Jaddai, Joel, and Benaiah. The clan name Nebo refers to the Babylonian god of wisdom and writing — that a Jewish family group bore this name suggests either that they adopted it as a geographical/clan identity (being from the region of Nebo), or that the name had been in use long enough to lose its pagan connotation. This is the last family group in the list and includes the names Joel (“YHWH is God”) and Benaiah (“YHWH has built”) — fitting theological names to close the covenant accountability record.
44: kol-ʾēlleh nāśᵉʾû nāšîm nāḵriyyôṯ — “all of these had taken foreign women.” The closing summary uses the same verb (nāśāʾ — to lift/take, standard Hebrew for marrying) as throughout the chapter. The final note — wᵉyēš mēhem nāšîm wayyāśîmû bānîm — “and some of them had wives by whom they had children” — acknowledges the human cost of the covenant action without softening the resolution. The book of Ezra ends here, without narrating the outcome — the reader is left with the completed accountability process and the implicit question of implementation. The open ending is not narrative incompetence but theological realism: covenant renewal is never a completed past event but an ongoing task.
